13. Revisiting Estimation (Bayesian)

13.1. Inferring distributions

Let be the quantity of interest, with prior and observational model . And suppose and are both finite.

Recall that with a cost function ,

(13.1)

  1. We have an inference engine that takes in and and produces an estimate based on the posterior belief . The prior and cost are known beforehand based on the application
  2. But suppose we don’t know the application ahead of time, or there are multiple applications. This leads us to needing a general purpose “inference” box.

Generalization: Our model will output a distribution over instead of a number . We will view “inference” itself as the estimate. Clearly the best distribution is .

Some examples of cost criterion

  1. (quadratic cost)
  2. (negative log loss, make as peaky as possible)

Definition 13.1 (Honest Bayes Engine). A cost function is proper if it leads to true beliefs being reported (“honest” Bayes engine), i.e.

(13.2)

Proposition 13.1. The quadratic loss and log-loss are both proper.
Proof
  1. Homework
  2. Gibb’s inequality

□

Definition 13.2 (Locality). A cost function is said to be local if it depends only on the value of at . for some
Proposition 13.2. The log-loss is local.
Theorem 13.1 (Characterization Theorem). [COOL] For , then log-loss is the only local, proper cost function.
Proof

□

Remark. “So if you want a scoring rule that rewards honest beliefs and only looks at the probability assigned to what actually happened, you essentially have no choice: use log-loss. That is why log-loss is so central in probabilistic prediction, ML, information theory, and Bayesian updating.” This is one of the many ways in which the log-loss function is special.

13.4. Cost Reduction

The cost reduction due to the data is called the mutual information.

(13.9)
